title: TaskFlow server gets slower throughout the day, requires daily restart
body:
Our self-hosted TaskFlow instance starts the day running fine but gets progressively slower. By late afternoon, page loads take 10+ seconds and the API times out. We've been restarting the server every evening for the past week. Memory usage on the server climbs from ~500MB at startup to 4GB+ by end of day. We have about 200 active users.

I've attached a screenshot of our Grafana dashboard showing the memory climb. The pattern is very consistent day-to-day.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
Thanks for the detailed report and the Grafana dashboard — the consistent daily memory climb pattern is very helpful.

To start narrowing this down: **Is this a recent change in behavior, or has it always been this way?** Specifically:

- What version of TaskFlow are you running, and did you recently upgrade?
- If this started recently, can you pinpoint roughly when (a deploy, a config change, a traffic increase)?
- Has your user count or usage pattern changed around the same time?

This will help determine whether we're looking at a regression from a recent change or a long-standing issue that's been masked.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: This is definitely a recent change. We upgraded from TaskFlow v2.2 to v2.3 about a week ago, and the memory issues started right after that. Before the upgrade, the server would run for weeks without needing a restart. Our user count hasn't changed, and we haven't made any other configuration changes — just the version upgrade. I actually checked the v2.3 changelog and noticed it mentions 'improved real-time notifications' as a new feature, which made me wonder if that's related since the timing lines up perfectly.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
Good catch on the v2.3 timing — that's a strong signal, and the real-time notifications feature is a very plausible suspect.

To dig into what's actually consuming that memory: **What runtime/platform is your TaskFlow instance running on (e.g., Node.js, Java, Python), and have you been able to look at any process-level diagnostics?** Specifically:

- If you run something like `top` or check your monitoring, is it the main TaskFlow process itself that's growing, or a child process / database connection pool?
- Do you see any errors or warnings in the TaskFlow server logs as the memory climbs — particularly anything related to WebSocket connections, event listeners, or notifications?
- Is the memory growth rate proportional to user activity (faster during peak hours, slower on weekends), or is it a steady linear climb regardless of load?

This will help determine whether we're looking at a connection leak, an unbounded cache, or something else entirely.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body:
It's running on our Ubuntu 22.04 VM with 8GB RAM. Looking at `top`, it's definitely the main TaskFlow process itself that's eating the memory — nothing else on the box is growing. I haven't noticed any explicit error messages in the logs about WebSocket connections or listeners, but I'll admit I haven't done a deep dive with grep specifically for those terms. I can check if that would help.

As for the growth rate — it's definitely proportional to user activity. During business hours when all ~200 users are active, the memory climbs noticeably faster. On weekends when only a handful of people are on, it still grows but much more slowly. That's actually a detail I hadn't thought about until you asked, but it's very consistent with what I see in Grafana. The weekday pattern is roughly 500MB at startup to around 3-4GB after a full 8-hour workday.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
The evidence is pointing strongly at the v2.3 real-time notifications feature — the timing, the correlation with active users, and the steady accumulation all fit a connection or event-listener leak pattern.

To test this theory directly: **Does TaskFlow have a configuration option to disable real-time notifications (or fall back to polling)?** If so, could you try running with notifications disabled for a day and see if the memory stays flat?

If there's no such toggle, a second approach: could you check how many active WebSocket connections the server reports? If your monitoring or logs show connection counts, I'd expect to see them climbing over the day and not dropping when users close their browsers. You can also try `ss -s` or `netstat` on the server to count established connections to the TaskFlow port — if connections are leaking, you'll see far more than your ~200 active users.

Also — one detail from earlier: what runtime does TaskFlow use (Node.js, Java, Python, etc.)? That'll help the dev team know exactly where to look in the code.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body:
I'm not sure if there's a config option to disable real-time notifications — I don't recall seeing one, but I haven't looked specifically for it. I'll check the docs and config file and can try that if it exists. That would be a great way to confirm the theory.

I just ran `ss -s` and I do see a surprisingly high number of established connections on the TaskFlow port — way more than the ~200 users we have active right now. I'll get an exact count and keep an eye on whether it keeps climbing throughout the day. That's suspicious for sure.

As for the runtime — I believe TaskFlow is written in Go. That's what I recall from the installation process at least.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body: [RESOLVED] {"title":"Memory leak in v2.3 real-time notifications: WebSocket/connection leak causes unbounded memory growth proportional to user activity","problem":"After upgrading from TaskFlow v2.2 to v2.3, the main TaskFlow process exhibits a steady memory leak, climbing from ~500MB at startup to 3-4GB+ over an 8-hour business day. Page loads degrade to 10+ seconds and API calls time out, requiring a daily server restart. The leak rate correlates directly with active user count.","root_cause_hypothesis":"The 'improved real-time notifications' feature introduced in v2.3 is not properly closing or cleaning up connections (likely WebSocket or long-poll connections) when users disconnect or navigate away. Each user session accumulates orphaned connections that are never released, causing both memory growth and connection count to climb unboundedly. The Go process holds these leaked connections and their associated buffers in memory.","reproduction_steps":["Deploy TaskFlow v2.3 on a server (Ubuntu 22.04, 8GB RAM used by reporter)","Allow ~200 users to use the application normally during business hours","Monitor memory usage of the main TaskFlow process over several hours","Observe memory climbing steadily from ~500MB toward 3-4GB over 8 hours","Run `ss -s` or `netstat` against the TaskFlow port and observe established connections far exceeding the active user count","Compare with v2.2 where the server runs for weeks without memory growth"],"environment":"Self-hosted TaskFlow v2.3 on Ubuntu 22.04 VM, 8GB RAM, Go runtime, ~200 active users during business hours","severity":"high","impact":"All self-hosted TaskFlow v2.3 instances with active users are affected. The server becomes unusable (10+ second page loads, API timeouts) by end of business day and requires daily restarts. This is a regression from v2.2 which ran stably for weeks.","recommended_fix":"Investigate the real-time notifications code path introduced or modified in v2.3. Specifically look for: (1) WebSocket or HTTP long-poll connection handlers that don't clean up on client disconnect, (2) missing `defer conn.Close()` or equivalent cleanup in Go connection handlers, (3) event listener or goroutine accumulation per connection without corresponding teardown, (4) connection registry or subscriber map that adds entries but never removes them on disconnect/timeout. Adding a connection idle timeout and proper disconnect handling should resolve the leak.","proposed_test_case":"Write a load test that simulates 50+ users connecting and disconnecting repeatedly over a 1-hour period. Assert that: (1) the active connection count never exceeds the number of currently connected clients by more than a small margin, (2) memory usage remains stable (within 20% of baseline) after all clients disconnect, and (3) goroutine count returns to baseline after clients disconnect.","information_gaps":["Exact number of established connections from `ss` (reporter confirmed 'way more than 200' but exact count not yet provided)","Whether TaskFlow has a configuration toggle to disable real-time notifications (reporter is checking)","Specific log entries when grepping for WebSocket/notification-related warnings (reporter hasn't done targeted grep yet)"]}
